mnemonics.title: Binary Search Steps
mnemonics.content: Binary Search: 'Find Middle, Compare, Half the Search Space!' - First find middle element, compare with target, then decide which half to search next.

mnemonics.title: Array Operation Complexities
mnemonics.content: 'Search Sort Traverse O(n), Insert Delete middle O(n), Access End O(1), Access Middle O(n)' - Helps remember common operation time complexities.

mnemonics.title: When to Use Arrays vs Linked Lists
mnemonics.content: 'A-rray for Random Access, L-inked List for Dynamic Insert' - Arrays for O(1) access, Linked Lists for frequent insertions/deletions.

acronyms.usage: Not directly for arrays, but useful for understanding stack operations on arrays
Useful when implementing queues using arrays
2D array storage method where rows are stored sequentially in memory
2D array storage method where columns are stored sequentially in memory

visualTips.visualization: Think of array indices as house numbers on a street. Each house (element) has a unique number (index), and you can directly go to any house if you know its number.
Imagine parking spaces in a row. Each car (element) occupies consecutive spots. You can find any car by counting from the start.
Like inserting a new person at the front of a queue - everyone must move back one position.
Think of searching for a word in a dictionary. You don't check every page - you open to the middle, see if your word is before or after, then repeat.

analogies.concept: Array as a Building Floor Plan
analogies.analogy: An array is like a building with numbered floors. You can go directly to any floor (index) using the elevator (direct access), unlike climbing stairs one by one (linked list traversal).

analogies.concept: Array Insertion as Movie Theater Seating
analogies.analogy: Inserting at the end of an array is like finding an empty seat in the last row - quick. Inserting at the beginning is like asking everyone to move one seat over - time-consuming.

analogies.concept: Binary Search as Word Search in Dictionary
analogies.analogy: Finding a word in a dictionary using binary search is like playing the 'higher/lower' game - you open to the middle, determine which half contains your word, and repeat until found.
